abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents require persistent memory to maintain personalization, factual continuity, and long-horizon reasoning, yet standard context-window and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines degrade over multi-session interactions. We present MemMachine, an open-source memory system that integrates short-term, long-term episodic, and profile memory within a ground-truth-preserving architecture that stores entire conversational episodes and reduces lossy LLM-based extraction. MemMachine uses contextualized retrieval that expands nucleus matches with surrounding context, improving recall when relevant evidence spans multiple dialogue turns. Across benchmarks, MemMachine achieves strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs: on LoCoMo it reaches 0.9169 using gpt4.1-mini; on LongMemEvalS (ICLR 2025), a six-dimension ablation yields 93.0 percent accuracy, with retrieval-stage optimizations -- retrieval depth tuning (+4.2 percent), context formatting (+2.0 percent), search prompt design (+1.8 percent), and query bias correction (+1.4 percent) -- outperforming ingestion-stage gains such as sentence chunking (+0.8 percent). GPT-5-mini exceeds GPT-5 by 2.6 percent when paired with optimized prompts, making it the most cost-efficient setup. Compared to Mem0, MemMachine uses roughly 80 percent fewer input tokens under matched conditions. A companion Retrieval Agent adaptively routes queries among direct retrieval, parallel decomposition, or iterative chain-of-query strategies, achieving 93.2 percent on HotpotQA-hard and 92.6 percent on WikiMultiHop under randomized-noise conditions. These results show that preserving episodic ground truth while layering adaptive retrieval yields robust, efficient long-term memory for personalized LLM agents.

citations.fullCitation: Charles Packer, Sarah Wooders, Kevin Lin, Vivian Fang, Shishir G. Patil, Ion Stoica, and Joseph E. Gonzalez. MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems. arXiv preprint arXiv:2310.08560, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a pioneering work that introduced an operating-system-inspired virtual memory hierarchy for LLMs. MemMachine's approach is contextualized against MemGPT's method of managing context by paging information between a main context and external storage.
citations.title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems

citations.fullCitation: Prateek Chhikara, Dev Khant, Saket Aryan, Taranjeet Singh, and Deshraj Yadav. Mem0: Building Production-Ready AI Agents with Scalable Long-Term Memory. arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.19413, 2025.
citations.justification: Mem0 is presented as a primary competing system used for direct quantitative comparison. The paper highlights MemMachine's superior efficiency, noting an 80% reduction in input tokens compared to Mem0's LLM-based extraction approach.
citations.title: Mem0: Building Production-Ready AI Agents with Scalable Long-Term Memory

citations.fullCitation: Adyasha Maharana, Dong-Ho Lee, Sergey Tulyakov, Mohit Bansal, Francesco Barbieri, and Yuwei Fang. Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents. arXiv preprint arXiv:2402.17753, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the LoCoMo benchmark, a central pillar of the paper's evaluation. Achieving a state-of-the-art score on LoCoMo is one of MemMachine's key claimed contributions and is used to validate its performance against other systems.
citations.title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents

citations.fullCitation: Di Wu, Hongwei Wang, Wenhao Yu, Yuwei Zhang, Kai-Wei Chang, and Dong Yu. Long-MemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory. arXiv preprint arXiv:2410.10813, 2024. Accepted at ICLR 2025.
citations.justification: This citation is crucial as it provides the LongMemEval benchmark, which forms the basis of the paper's extensive ablation study. This study systematically analyzes six optimization dimensions and leads to the key architectural insight that retrieval-stage optimizations dominate over ingestion-stage changes.
citations.title: Long-MemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory

citations.fullCitation: Tyler Barnes and Sam Bhagwat. Observational Memory: A Human-Inspired Memory System for AI Agents. Mastra Technical Report, 2026. https: //mastra.ai/research.
citations.justification: This work is discussed in detail as representing a contrasting architectural paradigm of 'observational memory' that compresses history rather than preserving raw ground truth. It is used to frame the core design tension between 'compression vs. preservation' and 'retrieval vs. stable context,' thereby clarifying MemMachine's architectural positioning.
citations.title: Observational Memory: A Human-Inspired Memory System for AI Agents

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Analysis: MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents" was authored by Shu Wang, Edwin Yu, Oscar Love, Tom Zhang, Tom Wong, Steve Scargall, and Charles Fan. All authors are affiliated with MemVerge, Inc.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Large language models (LLMs) form the computational basis for autonomous AI applications, yet they possess inherent limitations regarding static parameters and restricted context windows. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) addresses the injection of external knowledge but is primarily designed for static document collections, proving insufficient for the dynamic, bidirectional interactions required by persistent, personalized AI agents. Such agents necessitate a memory system capable of storing, organizing, recalling, and reasoning over past experiences, drawing inspiration from cognitive science models of short-term, long-term episodic, and semantic memory.

The current research landscape for AI agent memory systems includes several approaches. MemGPT explored virtual memory management for LLMs, conceptualizing LLMs as operating systems. Systems like Mem0 and Zep provide long-term memory layers, often relying on LLMs for data extraction, updating, and aggregation. However, this LLM-centric approach can lead to high operational costs, accuracy concerns due to probabilistic extraction, and accumulating errors over time. Other systems such as Memobase and LangMem offer structured or integrated memory solutions. More recently, Mastra introduced "observational memory," which compresses conversation history into an in-context log, favoring prompt cacheability over direct ground truth retrieval. MemOS represents a more ambitious architectural stance, proposing a full memory operating system that unifies plaintext, activation, and parametric memory types, though this requires direct access to model internals, limiting portability.

This diversity in approaches highlights a fundamental design tension: the trade-off between memory compression and ground truth preservation. Systems that aggressively compress (e.g., Mem0, Mastra) aim for smaller context windows and lower per-query costs but risk detail loss. Conversely, systems that preserve raw data prioritize factual integrity but require efficient retrieval mechanisms. MemMachine positions itself within this landscape by emphasizing ground-truth preservation while minimizing LLM dependence for routine operations and introducing advanced retrieval strategies, thereby addressing the brittleness of standard RAG workflows in multi-session, personalized agent interactions. The paper further contributes to the growing body of agent memory benchmarks, including LoCoMo, LongMemEval, and EpBench, which systematically evaluate agent memory capabilities.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary motivation for MemMachine stems from two fundamental limitations of current Large Language Model (LLM) agents when applied to persistent and personalized applications:
1.  **Static Parameters:** Once an LLM is trained, its knowledge base is fixed, preventing it from acquiring new information from ongoing interactions without costly fine-tuning or retraining.
2.  **Restricted Context Window:** LLMs operate within a finite context window, necessitating complex data curation and compression to fit relevant historical information, which often leads to the loss of critical context.

While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a prevalent method for injecting external knowledge, it typically operates on static document collections. This makes conventional RAG architectures inadequate for AI agents that require dynamic, bidirectional interactions to learn from and adapt to evolving user contexts across multiple sessions. Existing memory systems for AI agents, such as Mem0 and Zep, frequently rely on LLMs for routine memory operations like data extraction, update, aggregation, and deletion. This reliance introduces several challenges: high operational costs, potential accuracy degradation due to the probabilistic nature of LLM-based extraction, and the accumulation of errors over extended interactions, leading to "factual drift."

Against this background, MemMachine was developed with several key objectives:
*   **Ground-Truth Preservation:** To establish a memory architecture that stores raw conversational episodes without significant LLM-based transformation, thereby preserving factual integrity and minimizing the risk of information loss or distortion.
*   **Minimized LLM Dependence:** To reduce the reliance on LLMs for routine memory operations, reserving LLM calls for higher-level functions like summarization and abstraction, which aims to decrease operational costs and improve factual accuracy.
*   **Enhanced Retrieval Accuracy for Conversational Data:** To develop novel retrieval mechanisms, specifically "contextualized retrieval," that can effectively identify and present semantically relevant information from conversational history, even when direct keyword matches are insufficient.
*   **Support for Personalization:** To incorporate a profile memory system that can extract and maintain user preferences, facts, and behavioral patterns, enabling personalized agent interactions across sessions.
*   **Cost-Efficient Operation:** To achieve a substantial reduction in token usage compared to existing LLM-dependent memory systems, leading to lower inference costs and improved latency.
*   **Robust Multi-hop Reasoning:** To develop an intelligent retrieval agent capable of orchestrating complex retrieval strategies for multi-hop questions, where answers depend on information distributed across multiple memory entries.
*   **Open-Source Contribution:** To provide an open-source memory system for the AI agent community, offering a reproducible and configurable foundation for developing memory-augmented agents.

By addressing these objectives, MemMachine aims to provide a robust, cost-effective, and factually accurate memory system that enables AI agents to maintain personalization, conversational continuity, and high performance across long-horizon tasks and multi-session interactions.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

MemMachine implements a client-server architecture centered on a two-tier memory system: episodic memory (comprising short-term and long-term components) and profile memory (semantic).

**Core Design Principles:**
The system's fundamental principle is ground-truth preservation. It stores raw conversational episodes and indexes them at a fine-grained, sentence level. This approach aims to minimize reliance on LLMs for routine memory operations, thereby reducing cost and factual drift.

**Memory Types Implemented:**
1.  **Episodic Memory:** This component stores specific past experiences, with each conversational turn or interaction treated as an "Episode" with associated metadata (timestamp, producer, session ID, custom metadata).
    *   **Short-Term Memory (STM):** Maintains a configurable context window of the most recent episodes. It provides immediate conversational context and generates compressed summaries of session-level interactions via LLM-based abstraction when content exceeds the window.
    *   **Long-Term Memory (LTM):** Provides persistent, searchable storage for episodes that have exited the STM window. The ingestion pipeline for LTM involves:
        *   **Sentence Extraction:** Episodes are segmented into individual sentences using NLTK’s Punkt tokenizer for fine-grained indexing.
        *   **Metadata Augmentation:** Each sentence inherits metadata from its parent episode.
        *   **Relational Mapping:** Sentences are linked to their originating episodes to maintain provenance.
        *   **Embedding Generation:** Semantic embeddings are generated for each sentence using configurable embedding models.
    *   **Storage:** The original episodes, augmented sentences, and embeddings are persisted in a central database utilizing PostgreSQL (with pgvector for vector search) and Neo4j for graph-based relational storage.

2.  **Profile Memory (Semantic Memory):** This system extracts and maintains structured user-level facts and preferences from conversational data using LLMs. It stores distilled high-level user attributes (e.g., demographics, preferences, behavioral patterns) in SQL databases (PostgreSQL or SQLite), supporting both retrieval and updates to reflect the latest user state.

**Memory Search and Recall Workflow:**
MemMachine employs a staged recall pipeline (Figure 2). A query first checks STM, then proceeds to LTM.
*   **LTM Search:** Involves vector similarity search (ANN or exact matching) against sentence embeddings. Matched sentences are traced back to their originating episodes, and duplicates are removed.
*   **Episodic Search:** Coordinates results from STM and LTM, deduplicating overlapping episodes and sorting all results chronologically before returning them for agent context assembly.

**Key Innovations:**
*   **Contextualized Retrieval:** To address the challenge of conversational turns being highly interdependent, MemMachine expands nucleus episodes (located by embedding search) with immediate neighboring episodes (one preceding, two following) to form an episode cluster. These clusters are then reranked using a cross-encoder model before being provided to the LLM, ensuring the model receives necessary conversational context.
*   **Retrieval Agent:** For queries requiring multi-hop reasoning or multi-entity fan-out, MemMachine introduces an LLM-orchestrated Retrieval Agent (Figure 3). A `ToolSelectAgent` acts as a root router, classifying incoming queries into three structural types via an LLM call:
    *   **ChainOfQuery:** For multi-hop dependency chains, it performs iterative evidence accumulation (up to 3 iterations), involving retrieval, LLM-based sufficiency judgment, and query rewriting.
    *   **SplitQuery:** For single-hop multi-entity queries, it decomposes the query into 2–6 independent sub-queries, executes them concurrently, and pools results.
    *   **MemMachine (Direct):** For simple single-hop queries, routing directly to the baseline search with minimal overhead.
    *   **Multi-Query Reranking:** A key feature where the final reranker considers all queries used during retrieval (original, rewrites, sub-queries) to ensure comprehensive relevance in the final ranking.
    *   **Prompt Optimization:** Retrieval Agent prompts were tuned using the APO (Auto Prompt Optimization) algorithm from Agent Lightning.

**LLM Integration:**
LLMs are used strategically and sparingly: for STM summarization, profile extraction, and agent-mode inference. They are not used for per-message fact extraction, deduplication, or general memory management, contrasting with LLM-dependent systems.

**System Environment and Evaluation:**
*   **Environment:** MemMachine v0.3.x, Python 3.11, PostgreSQL + Neo4j on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS. Embedding models included OpenAI text-embedding-3-small and a reranker like AWS Cohere rerank-v3-5:0.
*   **Evaluation LLMs:** OpenAI gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-5, gpt-5-mini. Judge-LLM: gpt-4o-mini.
*   **Benchmarks:** LoCoMo, LongMemEval^S, HotpotQA hard, WikiMultiHop (randomized-noise setting), MRCR, EpBench.
*   **Metrics:** Primarily LLM Judge Score, complemented by BLEU and F1 scores.
*   **Reproducibility:** Benchmark scripts and configurations are open-sourced.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

MemMachine's evaluation across multiple benchmarks indicates its performance and efficiency in managing AI agent memory.

**LoCoMo Benchmark Results:**
On the LoCoMo benchmark, MemMachine achieved an overall LLM Judge Score of 0.9169 with `gpt-4.1-mini` in agent mode (Table 10). When compared against competing systems using `gpt-4o-mini` (for fair comparison with published baselines), MemMachine demonstrated the highest overall score of 0.8747, surpassing Memobase (0.7578), Zep (0.7514), Mem0 (0.6688), LangMem (0.5810), and OpenAI baseline (0.5290) (Table 11).
*   **Category-wise Performance:** MemMachine showed strong performance in single-hop queries (0.9465) and multi-hop queries (0.8759). Its temporal reasoning score was 0.7352, and open-domain score was 0.7083 with `gpt-4o-mini`. With `gpt-4.1-mini` in agent mode, temporal reasoning improved significantly to 0.9159.

**LongMemEval^S Ablation Study Results:**
A systematic ablation study on LongMemEval^S (n=500 questions) investigated the impact of six optimization dimensions, yielding an overall accuracy of 93.0% (C15, Table 12).
*   **Retrieval-Stage Dominance:** Optimizations at the retrieval stage contributed more substantially to accuracy than ingestion-stage changes (Table 13).
    *   **Retrieval Depth (k):** Increasing `k` from 20 to 30 yielded the largest improvement of +4.2 percentage points (from 0.870 to 0.912). However, further increases (e.g., to k=50 with GPT-5) could degrade performance (to 0.890), suggesting a balance between recall and noise. With `GPT-5-mini`, performance improved monotonically up to k=100 (0.930), indicating model robustness to context length.
    *   **Context Formatting:** Using appropriate line terminators to parse message boundaries improved accuracy by +2.0%.
    *   **Search Prompt Design:** Refined search prompts (Edwin3 over Edwin1) contributed +1.8% to accuracy.
    *   **User-Query Bias Correction:** Prepending "user:" to search queries to prioritize user messages yielded +1.4%.
*   **Ingestion-Stage Impact:** Sentence-level chunking provided a modest but consistent gain of +0.8%.
*   **Model-Prompt Co-optimization:** A notable finding was that `GPT-5-mini` outperformed `GPT-5` as the answer LLM by +2.6% (0.922 vs. 0.896) when paired with optimized prompts (Edwin3). This suggests that smaller, more instruction-following models can be more effective than larger models when prompt design is optimized.
*   **Per-Category Analysis:** Single-session information extraction (SSU, SSA) categories achieved near-saturated scores (0.98–1.00). Single-session preference (SSP) showed substantial improvement, rising from 0.700 to 0.933 with optimizations. Temporal reasoning (TR) improved from 0.798 to 0.932. Multi-session reasoning (MS) remained challenging, peaking at 0.872 (Table 14).

**Retrieval Agent Performance:**
The Retrieval Agent, designed for multi-hop reasoning, demonstrated improvements on complex question-answering tasks (Table 3).
*   **HotpotQA hard:** Achieved 93.2% overall accuracy and 95.5% gold-supporting-fact recall, a +2.0 and +1.3 percentage point improvement, respectively, over baseline MemMachine. The `ChainOfQuery` strategy demonstrated high recall (95.31%) on multi-hop questions (Table 4).
*   **WikiMultiHop (randomized-noise):** Reached 92.6% accuracy with `gpt-5-mini`, a +5.2 percentage point improvement over baseline MemMachine under challenging noise conditions.
*   **MRCR:** Achieved 81.4% accuracy and 99.4% recall, significantly outperforming the LLM baseline without memory (32.3%).
*   **Token Cost:** The Retrieval Agent incurs additional LLM calls for routing and strategy execution, increasing token usage (e.g., `ChainOfQuery` cost ~5,732 input tokens per question) (Table 5). However, this cost is bounded by design.

**Efficiency Analysis:**
MemMachine demonstrated significant efficiency gains.
*   **Token Reduction:** On LoCoMo, MemMachine used approximately 80% fewer input tokens than Mem0.
*   **Speed Improvements:** Memory add speed was ~75% faster than previous versions, and search operations were up to 75% faster.
*   **Cost-Accuracy Tradeoff:** The configuration `C12` (`GPT-5-mini`, k=20) achieved 0.922 accuracy with 2.58M input tokens, representing a Pareto-optimal point, outperforming other configurations that required more tokens for lower or marginally higher accuracy (Table 15).

These findings indicate that MemMachine's ground-truth-preserving architecture, combined with contextualized retrieval and LLM-orchestrated retrieval strategies, provides robust memory behavior for personalized AI agents, demonstrating strong accuracy and efficiency trade-offs across various benchmarks.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

MemMachine addresses critical limitations in existing Large Language Model (LLM) agent architectures, particularly concerning persistent personalization, factual continuity, and long-horizon task performance. Its ground-truth-preserving memory system, which stores raw conversational episodes and minimizes routine LLM-based extraction, contributes to the development of more reliable and trustworthy AI agents.

The core significance of this work lies in several areas:
*   **Enhanced Factual Integrity and Trustworthiness:** By preserving raw conversational episodes, MemMachine mitigates the risk of factual drift and accumulated errors common in systems relying heavily on LLM-based data extraction. This is particularly important for applications in sensitive domains requiring high factual accuracy, auditability, or compliance.
*   **Cost Efficiency and Scalability:** The substantial reduction in input token usage (approximately 80% less than Mem0) translates directly into lower operational costs for LLM-powered applications and improved inference latency. This efficiency makes deploying sophisticated, memory-augmented AI agents more economically viable at scale.
*   **Robust Personalization:** MemMachine's dual architecture of episodic memory (for specific past experiences) and profile memory (for distilled user preferences and facts) provides a robust foundation for true personalization. Agents can maintain conversational continuity, adapt to user preferences, and build trust by recalling past interactions, moving beyond generic LLM responses.
*   **Improved Retrieval for Complex Queries:** The introduction of contextualized retrieval significantly enhances recall for conversational data by ensuring that not just isolated relevant sentences but also their surrounding conversational context is retrieved. The Retrieval Agent further extends this capability to complex multi-hop and multi-entity queries, enabling agents to perform more sophisticated reasoning tasks that are challenging for single-shot vector retrieval.
*   **Architectural Insights:** The LongMemEval^S ablation study provides empirical evidence that retrieval-stage optimizations contribute more significantly to final accuracy than ingestion-stage changes. This suggests that memory system development efforts might be more effectively focused on robust retrieval mechanisms rather than extensive LLM-based preprocessing and compression, provided raw data integrity is maintained.
*   **Model-Prompt Co-optimization:** The finding that smaller, cost-efficient LLMs (e.g., GPT-5-mini) can outperform larger models (e.g., GPT-5) when co-optimized with appropriate prompts highlights the importance of tailored prompt engineering for specific models and tasks. This insight can lead to more efficient and performant deployments.
*   **Open-Source Contribution:** Releasing MemMachine as an open-source project under the Apache 2.0 license facilitates broader adoption, community-driven development, and transparent evaluation of agent memory systems. It provides a configurable and reproducible platform for researchers and developers.

In conclusion, MemMachine represents a significant step towards developing AI agents that can operate effectively over long durations, maintain deep personalization, and handle complex information retrieval tasks with high accuracy and cost efficiency. Its architectural choices and empirical validation offer valuable contributions to the evolving field of AI agent memory, providing a foundation for next-generation, memory-augmented intelligent systems.
overview:
## Memory Limitations in LLM Agents

Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed the landscape of artificial intelligence, serving as the core engine for autonomous agents. However, two primary architectural constraints limit their effectiveness in persistent, real-world applications: static parameters and a restricted context window. Once an LLM is trained, its knowledge is frozen, making it unable to learn from new interactions without expensive retraining. Furthermore, because LLMs can only process a finite amount of information at once (the context window), they struggle to maintain long-term coherence in extended conversations.

While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is commonly used to inject external data into LLM prompts, standard RAG is typically optimized for static document repositories. Personalized AI agents require a more dynamic, bidirectional memory system that can store, organize, and recall specific user interactions across multiple sessions. Existing solutions often rely heavily on LLMs to summarize or extract facts from every message, which introduces high operational costs, latency, and "factual drift"—a phenomenon where the probabilistic nature of LLMs leads to the gradual distortion of original information over time. MemMachine is designed to address these challenges by prioritizing the preservation of raw conversational data and minimizing unnecessary LLM intervention.

## Architectural Overview: Episodic and Profile Memory

MemMachine employs a client-server architecture built around two distinct types of memory, mirroring concepts from cognitive science: episodic memory and profile (semantic) memory.

**Episodic Memory**
Episodic memory stores specific past experiences. Each interaction or "turn" is treated as an episode, containing the raw text, a timestamp, the participant IDs, and session metadata. This memory is further divided into two tiers:
*   **Short-Term Memory (STM):** This maintains a configurable sliding window of the most recent episodes. When the window is exceeded, the system performs an "abstraction" step where the LLM generates a concise summary of the session to ensure immediate context is preserved without overwhelming the context window.
*   **Long-Term Memory (LTM):** When episodes exit the STM, they are persisted in the LTM. The ingestion process involves segmenting episodes into individual sentences using the NLTK Punkt tokenizer. These sentences are then converted into vector embeddings and stored in a database (PostgreSQL with pgvector) and a graph database (Neo4j) to maintain relational links between sentences and their parent episodes.

**Profile Memory**
While episodic memory captures "what happened," profile memory captures "who the user is." It uses LLMs to periodically extract structured facts, preferences, and behavioral patterns from conversations. This semantic layer allows the agent to maintain a high-level understanding of the user (e.g., demographics, interests, or professional goals) that persists across all sessions. This data is stored in standard SQL databases for structured querying and updates.

## The Principle of Ground-Truth Preservation

The defining characteristic of MemMachine is its commitment to ground-truth preservation. Unlike systems like Mem0 or Zep, which may transform or compress data immediately upon ingestion, MemMachine stores the raw conversational turns. 

By indexing information at the sentence level but retrieving the original episode, the system ensures that the LLM always has access to the exact words used by the user. This approach minimizes the "broken telephone" effect, where an LLM’s summary of a summary eventually loses the original meaning. Furthermore, by avoiding LLM calls for routine tasks like deduplication or simple fact extraction during ingestion, MemMachine significantly reduces token consumption and operational costs.

## Advanced Retrieval Mechanisms

In a conversational setting, a single relevant sentence often lacks the necessary context to be useful. For example, the sentence "I'll take the blue one" is meaningless without the preceding sentences describing the options. To solve this, MemMachine implements **Contextualized Retrieval**.

When the system identifies a "nucleus" episode $E_i$ through vector similarity search, it does not just return that single episode. Instead, it retrieves a cluster $C_i$ of surrounding episodes:

$$C_i = \{E_{i-1}, E_i, E_{i+1}, E_{i+2}\}$$

This cluster includes the immediate predecessor and two subsequent episodes, providing the "before and after" context of the interaction. These clusters are then passed through a cross-encoder reranker, which evaluates the relevance of the entire conversational block to the user's query before presenting the most pertinent information to the LLM.

## The Retrieval Agent for Complex Reasoning

For queries that cannot be answered with a single search—such as multi-hop questions where the answer depends on several disconnected pieces of information—MemMachine utilizes an LLM-orchestrated Retrieval Agent. This agent classifies incoming queries into different structural types:

1.  **ChainOfQuery:** Used for multi-hop dependencies. If a user asks a question $Q$ that requires intermediate information, the agent generates a sub-query $q_t$, retrieves information, and then uses that result $a_t$ to formulate the next sub-query. This iterative process continues until the agent determines it has sufficient evidence to provide a final answer.
2.  **SplitQuery:** Used for queries involving multiple independent entities. The agent decomposes the main query into 2–6 independent sub-queries, executes them in parallel, and aggregates the results.
3.  **Direct Search:** For simple, single-hop queries, the agent routes the request directly to the standard MemMachine search to minimize overhead.

The Retrieval Agent also employs a multi-query reranking strategy. It considers all variations of the query generated during the process (including rewrites and sub-queries) to ensure the final information provided to the response-generating LLM is as comprehensive as possible.

## Performance Benchmarks and Efficiency Analysis

The effectiveness of MemMachine was evaluated across several rigorous benchmarks, including LoCoMo (Long-Context Memory for AI Agents) and LongMemEval.

**Accuracy Results**
On the LoCoMo benchmark, MemMachine achieved an overall LLM Judge Score of 0.8747 using `gpt-4o-mini`. This significantly outperformed other memory systems like Zep (0.7514), Mem0 (0.6688), and LangMem (0.5810). When utilizing the advanced Retrieval Agent mode with `gpt-4.1-mini`, the score improved further to 0.9169, with particularly high performance in temporal reasoning (0.9159) and multi-hop queries (0.8759).

**Efficiency and Cost**
One of the most significant findings was MemMachine's efficiency. In the LoCoMo tests, it used approximately 80% fewer input tokens than Mem0. This is a direct result of the ground-truth-preserving strategy, which avoids the heavy LLM usage required by other systems for fact extraction and memory management. Additionally, the system demonstrated a 75% improvement in "memory add" speed and search latency compared to previous iterations.

## Key Findings from Ablation Studies

The researchers conducted a systematic ablation study using the LongMemEval benchmark ($n=500$ questions) to determine which components of the system contributed most to overall accuracy. The study revealed a crucial insight: **retrieval-stage optimizations are more impactful than ingestion-stage changes.**

Increasing the retrieval depth $k$ (the number of retrieved items) from $k=20$ to $k=30$ resulted in a 4.2% accuracy gain. Interestingly, for highly capable models like `gpt-5-mini`, performance continued to improve as $k$ increased to $k=100$, suggesting that modern "mini" models are remarkably robust to long contexts if the information is well-formatted. Other impactful retrieval-side optimizations included:
*   **Context Formatting:** Using clear line terminators to help the LLM parse message boundaries (+2.0%).
*   **Search Prompt Refinement:** Improving the instructions for the search agent (+1.8%).
*   **User-Query Bias:** Prepending "user:" to search queries to prioritize user intent (+1.4%).

Conversely, ingestion-side changes, such as the specific method of sentence-level chunking, provided a much smaller gain (+0.8%). This suggests that the quality of the "recall" process is the primary bottleneck in agent memory systems, rather than how the data is initially "memorized."

## Model-Prompt Co-optimization

A notable discovery in the evaluation was the relationship between model size and prompt design. The researchers found that `gpt-5-mini` actually outperformed the larger `gpt-5` model by 2.6% when paired with optimized prompts. This indicates that smaller, more instruction-following models can be more effective for memory-related tasks than their larger counterparts, provided the system's prompts are specifically tuned to the model's behavior.

## Conclusion

MemMachine offers a robust and efficient alternative to LLM-centric memory systems. By focusing on preserving the ground truth of conversational data and implementing sophisticated retrieval strategies like contextualized clustering and the Retrieval Agent, it achieves higher accuracy on complex, multi-hop, and temporal reasoning tasks. Its significant reduction in token usage makes it a practical choice for developers building personalized AI agents that require long-term memory without the prohibitive costs and factual drift associated with constant LLM-based data processing. Through its open-source contribution, MemMachine provides a scalable and transparent foundation for the next generation of memory-augmented AI applications.

keyInsights: Ground-truth preservation via fine-grained, sentence-level indexing is critical for minimizing factual drift and errors in long-term AI agent interactions.
Retrieval-stage optimizations, such as increased retrieval depth and improved context formatting, contribute more significantly to overall accuracy than ingestion-stage changes.
Smaller, instruction-following LLMs can outperform larger models when co-optimized with tailored prompts, indicating the importance of model-prompt co-design for efficiency and performance.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) have static parameters and restricted context windows, hindering their ability to learn dynamically and maintain long-term context.
Traditional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is insufficient for dynamic, bidirectional interactions required by persistent, personalized AI agents.
Existing LLM-centric memory systems for agents suffer from high operational costs, potential accuracy degradation due to probabilistic extraction, and accumulating 'factual drift'.
results: Achieved an LLM Judge Score of 0.8747 on the LoCoMo benchmark using `gpt-4o-mini`, outperforming Memobase, Zep, Mem0, LangMem, and OpenAI baseline.
Demonstrated approximately 80% fewer input tokens compared to Mem0 on LoCoMo, translating to significant cost reductions and improved inference latency.

The Retrieval Agent improved accuracy on HotpotQA hard by +2.0 percentage points and WikiMultiHop (randomized-noise) by +5.2 percentage points, showcasing enhanced performance on complex multi-hop reasoning tasks.
solution: MemMachine implements a client-server architecture with a two-tier memory system: episodic memory (short-term and long-term) and profile (semantic) memory, prioritizing ground-truth preservation by storing raw conversational episodes.
It minimizes LLM dependence for routine memory operations, using LLMs strategically for higher-level functions like summarization and profile extraction.

The system introduces novel retrieval mechanisms, including contextualized retrieval (expanding matched sentences with neighboring conversational turns) and an LLM-orchestrated Retrieval Agent for multi-hop queries.
summary.summary: MemMachine introduces a ground-truth-preserving memory system for personalized AI agents, utilizing a two-tier architecture that minimizes reliance on LLMs for routine operations. It achieved state-of-the-art performance on the LoCoMo benchmark with an LLM Judge Score of 0.8747 and reduced input token usage by approximately 80% compared to Mem0.

title: MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents
